peer_exemplars.reasoning: High openness and very high extraversion, combined with low agreeableness and strong anti-domination values, produce mild support for giving users control over algorithms, but low digital literacy limits the strength of this stance.

High libertarian anti-domination (97.78) and moderate GAL participatory-democracy preference (65.87) create mild support for user choice, but very low openness and high authoritarianism limit enthusiasm.
Clear support from high openness to values (83.17) favoring user autonomy and high conscientiousness seeking orderly choice, tempered by low anti-censorship (42.51) creating some hesitation.
Mild support reflecting high openness to experience (45.14) and high activity level (95.7) but tempered by low digital literacy (low) and low conscientiousness (35.19) reducing technical engagement.
High libertarian choice-support and openness to novel systems generate mild support, but low conscientiousness and distrust of regulatory follow-through limit conviction.

Openness and fairness support user choice, but low digital literacy and moderate trust in platforms limit strong backing.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High openness and high internal political efficacy, combined with strong anti-domination values and moderate trust in institutions, produce clear support for labelling synthetic media as a transparency measure, though low trust in others tempers it.

Strong dangerous-worldview (86.81) and high ethnic nationalism (96.78) support labelling as a defense against foreign manipulation, though anti-censorship libertarianism (91.48) prevents full endorsement.
Moderate support reflecting strong care-harm concerns about deception and high dutifulness, but limited by low trust in the labeling authority's competence or neutrality.
Strong support from high harm avoidance (90.85), high fact-checking behavior (77.72), high authoritarian law-and-order (99.14), and very high extraversion (99.81) pushing for clear labeling.
A dangerous-worldview perception of AI threats is tempered by strong anti-censorship and privacy concerns, resulting in a near-neutral stance leaning slightly positive.
Very high moral concerns about harm and purity, along with high authoritarianism, drive clear support for mandatory labelling to prevent deception and maintain social order.
Strong harm and fairness foundations drive support for labeling, and high conscientiousness ensures practical backing for this transparency measure.

Strong moral concern for honesty and fairness, combined with authoritarian punitiveness and high nationalism, support labeling to deter manipulation, with mild libertarian reservations about free expression.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Low agreeableness and low trust in others, combined with very high openness and low authoritarian submission, produce clear opposition to trusting mainstream journalism, as the person sees it as a tool of elite control.

Extremely low trust in science/experts (13.97), very low agreeableness-trust (20.51), and high populist anti-elitism (72.5) combine with a dismissive-avoidant attachment style to produce very strong distrust.
Strong distrust due to extremely low trust in institutions (mean 16.44), high right-wing authoritarian aggression (75.47), and a dangerous worldview seeing media as part of an untrustworthy elite.

Clear distrust stemming from very low system justification (0.33), low trust in institutions (44.01), very low political efficacy (45.67), and high protest participation (75.88) viewing mainstream media as elite-controlled.
Very low political trust, high populist anti-elitism, and a manichean worldview produce a clearly negative score reflecting suspicion that mainstream media is part of the corrupt establishment.
Moderate trust in institutions and high system justification, tempered by some populist anti-elitism, result in mild trust in mainstream journalism.

Extremely high trust in all institutions, including science and the judiciary, extends to mainstream journalism, and very low openness to alternative views reinforces this strong trust.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Strong anti-domination values and very high internal political efficacy, combined with low agreeableness and high openness, produce strong support for protecting researchers who expose platform misconduct, as a check on corporate power.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Very high trust in government and high system justification, combined with low dangerous-worldview and low authoritarian submission, produce mild opposition to distrusting elections, as the person generally trusts institutional processes.

Mild distrust reflecting very low trust in government (31.18) and judiciary (9.31) combined with high system justification (59.82) and dutifulness that partially curbs this skepticism.

Moderate system justification and low populism reduce distrust, but low political trust and moderate dangerous worldview create some skepticism.
Profoundly high trust in government and judiciary, combined with very low openness to anti-system narratives, makes this person strongly reject any suggestion that elections are untrustworthy.
